skill.name: investigating-codebases
skill.description: Guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through systematic exploration of unfamiliar repositories, features, entry points, imports, and architecture.
skill.summary: Structured codebase investigation for unfamiliar repositories and features.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, sensitive-file, and weak-crypto patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown examples and templates. The confirmed risk is moderate: bundled helper scripts perform read-only local filesystem traversal and external command calls for repository analysis, with no evidence of prompt injection, network exfiltration, destructive actions, or malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Read-Only Filesystem Traversal in Helper Scripts
medium_findings.description: The bundled scripts accept local paths and enumerate directories, entry points, imports, and file counts. This is useful for code investigation, but users should only run it on intended repositories because it can expose local file names and snippets to the AI session.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts clearly traverse user-selected paths and read source files for analysis. I found no network transfer, deletion, persistence, or credential harvesting behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Command and Weak-Crypto Hits Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Most static findings come from Markdown code fences, placeholder file paths, architecture examples, and words such as JWT or hash. These examples are not executable by themselves and do not show malicious intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown guidance or templates, not executable Ruby or shell backticks. The static analyzer appears to flag inline backticks and security-related terminology.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Configuration Discovery Guidance
low_findings.description: The skill tells agents to locate configuration files, including .env files. This can be legitimate investigation work, but marketplace users should avoid pasting secrets or unnecessary environment content into AI context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The guidance explicitly mentions .env files and configuration discovery. I did not find instructions to extract, transmit, or reveal secret values.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Commands in Directory Mapping Script
dangerous_patterns.description: The shell helper invokes basename, realpath, tree, find, sed, and wc against a selected project path. Arguments are quoted and operations are read-only, but this remains an external-command pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external commands are directly visible in the shell script. The script does not interpolate unquoted command text or execute discovered files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Local File Reads for Import Tracing
dangerous_patterns.description: The import tracer follows local imports and reads source files recursively. This is expected for dependency analysis, but it can bring unintended project content into analysis output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code reads files and recursively follows local imports from a user-specified file. No evidence of network output or command execution was found.

content.value_statement: Understanding an unknown repository takes time and can miss important relationships. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through structured codebase investigation.
actual_capabilities: Maps repository structure and identifies major source, test, config, and documentation areas.
Finds likely entry points across Node.js, Python, Go, Rust, Java, and web projects.
Traces local JavaScript, TypeScript, and Python imports from a selected file.
Provides checklists for feature, dependency, quality, testing, and security investigation.
Supplies templates for documenting file locations, execution flow, architecture, and next steps.
Recognizes common framework and architecture clues from project files and directory patterns.
limitations: The scripts perform static inspection only and do not understand runtime behavior.
Import tracing is limited to simple JavaScript, TypeScript, and Python import patterns.
Results depend on the files and paths the user allows the agent to inspect.
The skill can identify likely patterns, but humans must verify design conclusions.

use_cases.title: Onboard to a New Repository
use_cases.description: Build a map of project structure, entry points, main workflows, and important files before making changes.

use_cases.title: Explain a Feature Implementation
use_cases.description: Trace where a feature starts, which components it touches, and how data moves through the system.

use_cases.title: Prepare Technical Documentation
use_cases.description: Turn code exploration into clear notes with file references, architecture summaries, and follow-up questions.

prompt_templates.title: Map This Repository
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the investigating-codebases skill to map this repository. Identify project type, major folders, likely entry points, and the first files I should read.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the investigating-codebases skill to find where [feature name] is implemented. Include primary files, related files, and why each file matters.

prompt_templates.title: Trace a Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the investigating-codebases skill to trace how [workflow] runs from entry point to output. Show the steps, file references, and data transformations.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the investigating-codebases skill to analyze this codebase architecture. Identify patterns, dependencies, integration points, risks, and documentation gaps.

output: Primary location: authentication middleware and login handlers.
Related files: token validation, session storage, user repository, and route definitions.
Flow: request enters routing, passes validation, checks credentials, creates session state, then returns a response.

output_examples.input: Explain how the build process works.
output: The response identifies build configuration files and scripts.
It orders the build steps from setup through compilation and output generation.
It notes environment differences, generated artifacts, and follow-up files to inspect.

output_examples.input: Give me an architecture overview before I edit this repository.
output: The response summarizes project type, folder responsibilities, entry points, shared utilities, and test locations.
It highlights likely framework conventions and areas that need human verification.

best_practices: Start with repository structure and documentation before reading deep implementation files.
Limit inspection to the repository or feature area needed for the current task.
Ask for file references, confidence levels, and open questions in every investigation result.
anti_patterns: Do not run helper scripts on directories that contain unrelated secrets or personal files.
Do not accept architecture conclusions without checking the cited files.
Do not paste full environment files, credentials, or private production data into the AI session.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps an AI assistant explore unfamiliar code and explain structure, features, entry points, imports, and architecture.

faq.question: Does it execute application code?
faq.answer: The included scripts perform static inspection. They do not run the target application or call its business logic.

faq.question: Can it find where a feature is implemented?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides search, file reading, import tracing, and documentation of related components.

faq.question: Which languages are supported?
faq.answer: The guidance covers many languages. The import tracer focuses on JavaScript, TypeScript, and Python.

faq.question: Is it safe to use on private repositories?
faq.answer: Use it only on repositories you intend to share with the AI session. Avoid exposing secrets or unrelated files.

faq.question: Does it replace human code review?
faq.answer: No. It produces investigation notes and likely relationships. A human should verify important technical decisions.
